paper_metadata.title: Globally representative evidence on the actual and perceived support for climate action
paper_metadata.authors: Peter Andre; Teodora Boneva; Felix Chopra; Armin Falk
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: Global (125 countries); carbon-tax validation evidence from United States only
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: 129,902 individuals aged 15+ from 125 countries in Gallup World Poll 2021/2022; separate US online survey of ~2,000 respondents used for validation analyses.
exact_tax_terms_used: a carbon tax on fossil fuels
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: The main global survey measures general demand for more climate action. In a separate representative US online survey, this general demand is shown to correlate strongly with support for specific policies, including a carbon tax on fossil fuels.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: General demand for more political action on climate
determinants.definition: Binary indicator that respondent thinks the national government should do more to fight global warming.
determinants.mechanism: Reflects stronger concern about climate change and desire for ambitious policy, which translates into higher stated support for adopting a carbon tax on fossil fuels.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: US online validation survey (Andre et al. 2022); support for "a carbon tax on fossil fuels" measured on a 4-point Likert scale.
determinants.outcome_variable: Stated support for a carbon tax on fossil fuels (specific climate policy item).
determinants.evidence_quote: "the general demand for more political action is strongly correlated with demand for specific climate policies, such as a carbon tax on fossil fuels" (ρ between 0.49 and 0.59, all P<0.001).
determinants.page_or_section: Main text p.254; Methods – Validation; Supplementary Table 3
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Correlation between general demand for more political action and support for specific policies (incl. carbon tax) ρ=0.49–0.59; all P<0.001; N=1,996; US sample.
determinants.notes: Carbon-tax item appears only in separate US validation survey, not in global Gallup sample; exact ρ for carbon-tax item not reported separately.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Carbon-tax evidence limited to US validation sample
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: The main globally representative survey does not include a carbon-tax item; only the US online survey (Andre et al. 2022) measures support for "a carbon tax on fossil fuels" and reports correlations with general demand for more political action.
